item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite my headaches with the dialogue, this is alright. Congratulations on the first article.

item.title: Re: I dont Think I’ll ever understand what gets highly rated
item.content:encoded: But… it is finished.

item.title: I dont Think I’ll ever understand what gets highly rated
item.content:encoded: There’s literally nothing here besides “people get turned into snowmen, don’t ask why”. It’s as if you told me you’d bring me toast and you put frozen bread in front of me. Put in the work to finish it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this kind of SCP! It made me think would I rather die a human being or live on through a bizarre existence as something else. Something unnatural. In this case a Snowman. I particularly like that the farmer isn't sure yet how he feels about his new "life." I certainly hope you will expand on the lore of this SCP in the future.

That said, I do have a question. You wrote that no one can pass through the barrier but you also stated that interviews are done beyond it? I'm not sure I understand. I may just be reading it wrong though. Also, I feel like the child's dialog is a little off putting. I understand that you wanted him to speak like a young child but it didn't feel natural in my opinion.

All in all though I think this is a great article. +1 for sure!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess I wanted the character to talk like a child would talk, but I understand how that can be off putting. I had an earlier rendition of that dialogue that was less cheesy that I might reinstall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From what I can see, the last interview was most likely meant to be disturbing, but the way it was worded

SCP-5801-1-B: Well, I had a thingy called a brain tumor, and mommy and daddy didn't like that! So they took me to these people called the-

makes it hard for me to take it seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I think that's a common criticism I've received. I guess I left it underdeveloped to keep it mysterious, but I understand how the article could use more beef.

Thank you for your feedback, it helps me make sure my future articles are better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this to be underdeveloped. I totally understand the desire and need for an article to punch and just get out; you don't want to overstay the welcome. But in that same notion, I have to feel that initial attachment.

I was introduced to a mystery and then in two very short interviews it was gone and we're done. The snowman melting to leave behind a heart is a powerful image to work off of, dialogue aside, but you have to let the story stretch its legs and I'm not sure you've done that.

A good effort, but I really just want more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand your criticisms. I guess you can't please everyone. Your feedback is greatly appreciated, and I'll take it into account for my future articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In order for shock horror to be truly effective, it must earn it.

This does not, in any way. The dialogue is cliché and campy, coming off more like poorly-crafted creepypasta than of any real substance. Additionally, Morrissey is nothing more than a foil that exists to allow your -# to say whatever it is you want them to say, and it's painfully obvious.

I expect more from things written for publication here.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to change the measurements to the diameter to avoid confusion.

Thanks for the +1 btw and the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Approximately how big is the region? I got thrown off with the description that the circumference of the barrier is 15.7 meters, as that made me think the region is circular with a radius of about 3 meters. Is 15.7 meters meant to be the depth or thickness of the barrier?

Also, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well it sucks that you didn't enjoy the article, but your reasoning is valid. Thank you for the honest feedback, it really helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending here was effective, but only by virtue of using a shock-horror type of writing that left a really bad taste in my mouth. There's nothing in the rest of the article that feels like it builds up to it (beyond establishing the necessary logic) or otherwise elicits any sort of response in me. This ultimately just ends up feeling very hollow. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. I realized that if I changed it, it would just cause bigger problems lmao.

EDIT: Big fan of your work PeppersGhost. SCP-4000 is one of my favorite articles on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FWIW I think it works fine as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I put it back in. I don't want to overpolish the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I edited out the frosted abyss part thanks to your feedback. Hopefully this improves the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is understandable. I guess I was going for more of an emotional punch than an something unpredictable, but your criticisms are still valid. Thank you for your feedback. I'm glad you enjoyed reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding the Frozen Abyss thing. It makes it predictable.

On the other hand, that part is easy to predict even without the Frozen Abyss. If they're not allowed to tell, it's to be expected that someone will tell and something horrifying will happen.

It's also a bit bare bones, and a tad reminiscent of scp-2200.

With that said, I like the concept. I enjoyed reading it, but I would definitely like to see something more done with it. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is a valid criticism. I mainly included that detail for the sake of build up to the climax, but I can see how it could maybe be a bit too revealing.

And honestly, exploring what I suggested is a pretty good idea. I might make a tale or even maybe another SCP that relates to this one later down the line.

Thank you for the upvote and critique, I'm glad that you like it :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

Though I'll admit: once they mentioned the "Frozen Abyss", I think I immediately knew where things would lead up to.

That, plus the fact that one thing I know: being in a kid in the SCPverse lowers your chance of survival by a million.

+1 nonetheless

Wonder if you are going to work on what you have suggested more: I have a feeling that the Frost Gods will not take kindly to the Foundation snooping around (given the fact that no more snowpeople are happening)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks dude, I appreciate it. Glad the ending had its intended effect on you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yikes, it was so wholesome until the end
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much, cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats on the first successful article! Here's to many more! 🍻

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image used for this article can be found here. The author is Øyvind Holmstad. The image is published under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International liscense.

I want to thank joeydotrawr and tupacofficial for reviewing this concept all the way back in October of last year. Y’all really helped solidify this concept into something way better than what I originally was going to do. I also want to thank Discord users Pedagon and fwimmygoat for giving me feedback on the completed draft.

I originally started the draft for this article back in October, and for some reason didn’t get back to it until just recently. I’m glad that I got back to it though.

EDIT: Wow, thanks for all the upvotes! To any other new SCP authors out there, you are going to get an article to survive on the wiki if you keep trying! Don't worry, my first few concepts were horrible (Exploding watch, Infinite ketchup). Keep getting feedback and reading essays on the Guide Hub. I cannot preach enough how important feedback and research is for this kind of thing.

EDIT: Reversed a change I made regarding the "Frosted Abyss." The change ended up making a plot hole that I did not want to fill.
